title: Intensive Care Management MCQ – Chapter 40
source: Short Textbook of Anesthesia – Ajay Yadav

questions.question: The first intensive care unit (ICU) was started in which year?
questions.explanation: According to the textbook, the first intensive care unit (ICU) was started in 1953.
questions.reference: Ch 40, Section: Intensive Care Unit, Page 277

questions.question: What percentage of hospital beds should be allocated to the ICU?
questions.explanation: Total number of beds in ICU should be 10% of hospital beds, with each bed having 20 m² floor area.
questions.reference: Ch 40, Section: Intensive Care Unit, Page 277

questions.question: Acute respiratory failure is considered to be present when pO₂ is less than:
questions.explanation: Acute respiratory failure is considered to be present if pO₂ is less than 60 mm Hg, pCO₂ over 50 mm Hg and pH less than 7.30.
questions.reference: Ch 40, Section: Acute Respiratory Failure – Definition, Page 277

questions.question: Elevated pCO₂ in the presence of normal pH constitutes:
questions.explanation: Elevated pCO₂ in presence of normal pH constitutes chronic respiratory failure, as pH is compensated by renal tubular reabsorption of bicarbonate.
questions.reference: Ch 40, Section: Acute Respiratory Failure – Definition, Page 277

questions.question: Type II respiratory failure is characterized by all of the following EXCEPT:
questions.explanation: In Type II (hypercapnic ventilatory failure), pCO₂ is >50 mm Hg, pO₂ is usually normal, and pAO₂–paO₂ gradient is normal. Increased pAO₂–paO₂ gradient is a feature of Type I failure.
questions.reference: Ch 40, Section: Classification, Page 277–278

questions.question: Which of the following is a cause of Type II (hypercapnic ventilatory) respiratory failure?
questions.explanation: Guillain-Barré syndrome is a disorder affecting signal transmission to respiratory muscles and is a cause of Type II failure. The other options are causes of Type I failure.
questions.reference: Ch 40, Section: Type II Failure, Page 277–278

questions.question: Oxygen therapy is helpful in improving oxygen saturation in all types of hypoxia EXCEPT:
questions.explanation: Oxygen therapy is helpful in improving oxygen saturation in all types of hypoxia except histotoxic hypoxia. Maximum benefit is seen in hypoxic hypoxia.
questions.reference: Ch 40, Section: Supplemental Oxygen, Page 278

questions.question: Ideally, inspired oxygen concentration (FiO₂) should not exceed more than what percentage to avoid oxygen toxicity?
questions.explanation: Ideally, inspired oxygen concentration (FiO₂) should not exceed more than 50%, otherwise oxygen toxicity can occur.
questions.reference: Ch 40, Section: Supplemental Oxygen, Page 278

questions.question: Which of the following is an indication for mechanical ventilation based on pulmonary function?
questions.explanation: Indications for mechanical ventilation based on pulmonary functions include respiratory rate >35/min, vital capacity <15 mL/kg, VD/VT >0.6, and tidal volume <5 mL/kg.
questions.reference: Ch 40, Section: Mechanical Ventilation – Indications, Page 278

questions.question: On the basis of blood gas analysis, mechanical ventilation is indicated when pO₂/FiO₂ is less than:
questions.explanation: Mechanical ventilation is indicated when pO₂/FiO₂ <250 mm Hg (normal >400).
questions.reference: Ch 40, Section: Mechanical Ventilation – Indications, Page 278

questions.question: The chief advantage of volume preset (volume controlled) ventilation is:

options.B: Assurance of delivery of preset tidal volume

questions.explanation: The chief advantage of volume preset breath is assurance of delivery of preset tidal volume and hence decreased chances of hypoventilation; however, the chief disadvantage is increased chances of barotrauma.
questions.reference: Ch 40, Section: Ventilators, Page 279

questions.question: The usual initial tidal volume setting on a ventilator is:
questions.explanation: The usual initial tidal volume set on ventilator is 6–8 mL/kg.
questions.reference: Ch 40, Section: Initial Setting of Ventilator, Page 279

questions.question: The normal inspiratory to expiratory (I:E) ratio set on a ventilator is:
questions.explanation: The usual initial I:E ratio set on ventilator is 1:2.
questions.reference: Ch 40, Section: Initial Setting of Ventilator, Page 279

questions.question: The major disadvantage of controlled-mode ventilation (CMV) is:

options.C: Intrathoracic pressure always remains positive, decreasing venous return and cardiac output

questions.explanation: The major disadvantage of CMV is that the intrathoracic pressure always remains positive, decreasing the venous return and cardiac output.
questions.reference: Ch 40, Section: Modes of Ventilation – CMV, Page 279

questions.question: In Assist-Control (A/C) ventilation, if the preset rate is 10 breaths/min and the patient's spontaneous rate is 12, the ventilator will:

options.B: Assist 10 breaths and deliver 2 in control mode

questions.explanation: In A/C mode, if the patient's spontaneous rate exceeds the set rate (e.g., 12 vs set rate of 10), the ventilator will assist all 12 breaths, working in assist mode only.
questions.reference: Ch 40, Section: Modes of Ventilation – A/C, Page 280

questions.question: Which of the following is an advantage of SIMV over CMV?

options.B: Less hypotension due to spontaneous breaths allowing good venous return

questions.explanation: Advantages of SIMV over CMV include less hypotension (as good venous return occurs during spontaneous breaths), no need for heavy sedation/muscle relaxants, and less sense of breathlessness.
questions.reference: Ch 40, Section: Modes of Ventilation – SIMV, Page 280

questions.question: In Inverse Ratio Ventilation (IRV), the I:E ratio is reversed to:
questions.explanation: In IRV, the normal I:E ratio of 1:2 is reversed to 2:1. Doubling the inspiratory time doubles the gas exchange time.
questions.reference: Ch 40, Section: Modes of Ventilation – IRV, Page 280

questions.question: In Pressure Support Ventilation (PSV), the ventilator cycles to expiration when the predetermined flow falls below:
questions.explanation: In PSV, the ventilator will cycle to expiration once the predetermined flow falls below 25% (flow cycled).
questions.reference: Ch 40, Section: Modes of Ventilation – PSV, Page 280

questions.question: The most commonly used dual mode of ventilation in India is:
questions.explanation: The most commonly used dual mode in India is pressure-regulated volume control (PRVC). PRVC can be considered as the mode of choice for ventilation in current day critical care practice.
questions.reference: Ch 40, Section: Dual Mode Ventilation, Page 281

questions.question: High-frequency jet ventilation operates at a frequency of:
questions.explanation: High-frequency jet ventilation operates at a frequency of 100–300 cycles/min with gases at high pressure of 60 psi. It is the most commonly used mode for high frequency ventilation.
questions.reference: Ch 40, Section: High-Frequency Ventilation, Page 281

questions.question: Which is the most commonly used mode for high-frequency ventilation?
questions.explanation: High-frequency jet ventilation is the most commonly used mode for high frequency ventilation, with frequency of 100–300 cycles/min.
questions.reference: Ch 40, Section: High-Frequency Ventilation, Page 281

questions.question: The ideal PEEP is defined as the PEEP which maintains oxygen saturation >90% without decreasing the cardiac output significantly. The usual range is:
questions.explanation: The ideal PEEP which maintains oxygen saturation >90% without decreasing the cardiac output significantly is usually 5–12 cm H₂O.
questions.reference: Ch 40, Section: PEEP, Page 282

questions.question: CPAP (Continuous Positive Airway Pressure) is essentially:

options.B: PEEP given to a nonintubated patient
options.C: Pressure support ventilation through a mask

questions.explanation: CPAP is described as a misnomer. It is just PEEP given to a nonintubated patient, i.e., the noninvasive method of giving PEEP through a tight fitting mask.
questions.reference: Ch 40, Section: CPAP, Page 282

questions.question: In BIPAP, the typical inspiratory positive airway pressure (IPAP) setting is:
questions.explanation: Typical BIPAP settings are 8–20 cm H₂O positive pressure during inspiration (IPAP) and 5 cm H₂O during expiration (EPAP).
questions.reference: Ch 40, Section: BIPAP, Page 282

questions.question: What is the most common indication of NIPPV in the acute setting?
questions.explanation: In the acute setting, NIPPV is most frequently applied for acute exacerbation of COPD. In the chronic setting, it is most effectively used for sleep apnea syndrome.
questions.reference: Ch 40, Section: NIPPV – Common Indications, Page 282

questions.question: What is the major problem during NIPPV?
questions.explanation: Leakage is a major problem during NIPPV. Significant leaks can cause hypoventilation and are best avoided by using tight fitting masks.
questions.reference: Ch 40, Section: NIPPV – Complications, Page 283

questions.question: For weaning from ventilator, the Rapid Shallow Breathing Index (RSBI) should be:
questions.explanation: RSBI = Respiratory rate (breaths/min) / Tidal volume (in liters). RSBI should be <100 for successful weaning.
questions.reference: Ch 40, Section: Weaning from Ventilator, Page 283

questions.question: Weaning from ventilator is possible in all modes of ventilation EXCEPT:
questions.explanation: It is possible to wean a patient in any mode of ventilation except control mode ventilation.
questions.reference: Ch 40, Section: Method of Weaning, Page 283

questions.question: The incidence of pulmonary barotrauma during mechanical ventilation is:
questions.explanation: The incidence of barotrauma is 7–10%. It may manifest as pneumothorax, pneumomediastinum, pneumopericardium, pneumoperitoneum, bronchopleural fistula and air embolism.
questions.reference: Ch 40, Section: Complications of Mechanical Ventilation, Page 283–284

questions.question: The most common source of nosocomial infections in ICU is:
questions.explanation: The most common source of nosocomial infections in ICU are urinary tract infections accounting for 35–40% of total ICU infections, followed by pulmonary (25–30%) and bloodstream infections (20%).
questions.reference: Ch 40, Section: Complications – Infections, Page 284

questions.question: Ventilator-associated pneumonia (VAP) is defined as pneumonia developing in patients on ventilators for more than:
questions.explanation: VAP is defined as pneumonia developing in patients on ventilators for >48 hours with no evidence of preintubation pneumonia. The incidence of VAP is around 20%.
questions.reference: Ch 40, Section: Complications – VAP, Page 284

questions.question: The simplest and most cost-effective method to decrease ventilator-associated pneumonia is:

options.A: Use of subglottic suctioning endotracheal tubes
options.C: Positioning head of bed at 30 degrees

questions.explanation: Positioning the head of the bed at 30 degrees decreases gastroesophageal reflux and proves to be the simplest and most cost-effective method to decrease VAP.
questions.reference: Ch 40, Section: Complications – VAP, Page 284

questions.question: Shock is classified into how many major types?
questions.explanation: Shock is classified into four major types: hypovolemic, cardiogenic, distributive, and obstructive.
questions.reference: Ch 40, Section: Shock – Classification, Page 285

questions.question: In cardiogenic shock, the pulmonary capillary wedge pressure (PCWP) in left ventricular failure is typically:
questions.explanation: In cardiogenic shock, poor pump function leads to increased CVP and increased PCWP. In LVF, PCWP is >25 mm Hg.
questions.reference: Ch 40, Section: Shock – Hemodynamic Parameters, Page 285

questions.question: The hallmark hemodynamic finding in distributive shock is:
questions.explanation: In distributive shock, poor vascular tone (decreased SVR) is the hallmark. Decreased SVR decreases the afterload, increasing the cardiac output.
questions.reference: Ch 40, Section: Shock – Hemodynamic Parameters, Page 285

questions.question: In hemorrhagic shock, the current guidelines recommend maintaining systolic blood pressure (SBP) between:
questions.explanation: Current guidelines favor hypotensive resuscitation, maintaining SBP between 80–100 mm Hg and MAP between 60–65 mm Hg to prevent disruption of clots and increased bleeding.
questions.reference: Ch 40, Section: Management of Shock – Early Goals, Page 286

questions.question: The concept of combining hypotensive resuscitation and hemostatic resuscitation is called:
questions.explanation: The newer concept of hypotensive and hemostatic resuscitation is called balanced resuscitation.
questions.reference: Ch 40, Section: Management of Shock – Early Goals, Page 286

questions.question: Which parameter is considered the most reliable to assess fluid status and titrate fluid therapy?
questions.explanation: Stroke volume variation is considered most reliable to assess fluid status and titrate fluid therapy; however, due to technical feasibility and cost, CVP is still most commonly used.
questions.reference: Ch 40, Section: Treatment – Fluids, Page 286

questions.question: The most preferred crystalloid for fluid resuscitation in shock is:
questions.explanation: Ringer is the most preferred crystalloid as it is more physiological, except for hypochloremic alkalosis and brain injury where normal saline is preferred.
questions.reference: Ch 40, Section: Treatment – Choice of Fluids, Page 287

questions.question: The vasopressor of choice for septicemic shock is:
questions.explanation: Noradrenaline is the vasopressor of choice for septicemic shock. Phenylephrine is first-line for neurogenic shock and ephedrine is most preferred for spinal shock.
questions.reference: Ch 40, Section: Treatment – Drugs, Page 287

questions.question: Steroids are contraindicated in which type of shock?
questions.explanation: Steroids are contraindicated in cardiogenic shock as they can alter the healing process of myocardium. They are effective for septicemic shock refractory to fluids and vasopressors.
questions.reference: Ch 40, Section: Treatment – Drugs, Page 287

questions.question: The vein of choice for parenteral nutrition is:
questions.explanation: Due to large diameter (and hence less chances of thrombophlebitis due to lipid emulsions), subclavian is the vein of choice for parenteral nutrition.
questions.reference: Ch 40, Section: Nutritional Support – Parenteral Route, Page 288

questions.question: Out of total energy requirement, what percentage should be provided by carbohydrates?
questions.explanation: Out of total energy required, 60–70% should be provided by carbohydrates (dextrose) and 30–40% by fats. Amino acids are given as supplement, never as a substrate for source of energy.
questions.reference: Ch 40, Section: Substrates for Providing Energy, Page 288

questions.question: Sepsis is the leading cause of death in ICU. The most common cause of death in septicemia is:
questions.explanation: ARDS and renal failure are the most common causes of death in septicemia.
questions.reference: Ch 40, Section: Sepsis, Page 290

questions.question: Pulmonary edema develops when pulmonary artery occlusion pressure (left atrial pressure) rises to more than:
questions.explanation: Cardiogenic pulmonary edema develops when pulmonary artery occlusion pressure (left atrial pressure) rises to more than 25 mm Hg (or 30 cm H₂O).
questions.reference: Ch 40, Section: Pulmonary Edema – Hemodynamic Type, Page 290

questions.question: According to the Berlin definition, severe ARDS is defined as PaO₂/FiO₂:

options.A: ≤300 mm Hg with PEEP ≥5 cm H₂O
options.B: ≤200 mm Hg with PEEP ≥5 cm H₂O
options.C: ≤100 mm Hg with PEEP ≥5 cm H₂O
options.D: ≤50 mm Hg with PEEP ≥10 cm H₂O

questions.explanation: Berlin definition classifies ARDS as: Mild (PaO₂/FiO₂ ≤300), Moderate (≤200), and Severe (≤100 mm Hg), all with PEEP or CPAP ≥5 cm H₂O.
questions.reference: Ch 40, Section: ARDS – Berlin Definition, Page 291

questions.question: The most common cause of ARDS is:
questions.explanation: Sepsis is the most common cause of ARDS.
questions.reference: Ch 40, Section: Causes of ARDS, Page 291

questions.question: Carbon monoxide poisoning is usually defined as carboxyhemoglobin greater than what percentage in blood?
questions.explanation: Carbon monoxide poisoning is usually defined as carboxyhemoglobin >20% in blood. The affinity of CO to bind hemoglobin is 240 times that of oxygen.
questions.reference: Ch 40, Section: Carbon Monoxide Poisoning, Page 292

questions.question: The normal anion gap is:
questions.explanation: Normal anion gap = 8–16 mM/L (average 12 mM/L). Anion gap = Sodium concentration – (chloride + bicarbonate concentration). It is mainly constituted by plasma albumin.
questions.reference: Ch 40, Section: Anion Gap, Page 294

questions.question: The most common cause of metabolic acidosis in anesthesia is:

options.C: Lactic acidosis due to tissue hypoxia

questions.explanation: Lactic acidosis is the most common cause of metabolic acidosis in anesthesia and is due to tissue hypoxia. It is associated with increased anion gap.
questions.reference: Ch 40, Section: Metabolic Acidosis – Increased Anion Gap, Page 295
